full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|3.16|> This morning we find ourselves on the level place for the Sermon on the Plain.<|10.32|> <|10.32|> Matthew's version of this teaching, the Sermon on the Mount, feels more Colorado appropriate.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I am favorably disposed to Luke's telling of this event.<|5.72|> <|6.82|> There is a more clearly practical component to this lesson for Jesus' disciples,<|14.56|> <|15.30|> his disciples then and across the ages.<|18.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In Luke, Jesus does not talk about the poor in spirit.<|6.28|> <|7.44|> He talks about the poor.<|8.70|> <|9.94|> Jesus does not talk about those who hunger and thirst for righteousness.<|13.84|> <|14.84|> He talks about the hungry.<|16.66|> <|18.00|> They are blessed.<|20.06|> <|21.14|> And in Luke, there are the woes.<|24.16|> <|25.12|> Oh my, the woes.<|28.12|> <|28.12|> The woes.<|28.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus has come down from the mountain, from that lofty place where, closer to God, he had gone to pray and choose his twelve apostles from among the great crowd of disciples.<|16.56|> <|17.12|> He is now literally mobbed as people are reaching out to touch him and be cured of all that ails them.<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is in this moment surrounded by people who are desperate for healing<|4.82|> <|4.82|> that he looks to his disciples and teaches them about blessings and woes.<|13.08|> <|14.32|> And this teaching from Jesus isn't really all that surprising.<|19.48|> <|19.96|> If we remember that the Magnificat,<|22.94|> <|22.94|> the song of Mary that told of a world order turned upside down,<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is in Luke's gospel. Luke continually points back to Mary's words and how Jesus lived out Mary's<|12.10|> <|12.10|> prophecy. These blessings and woes demonstrate a reversal in the world order, in cultural priorities.<|22.26|> <|22.26|> Amen.<|22.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This simply isn't the way things work.<|4.36|> <|5.42|> There's a direct through line from Mary's song to this sermon on the plain.<|11.70|> <|12.40|> They both express a message of hope for the oppressed and a critique of societal power structures.<|20.02|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> years.<|1.08|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, there is often an all-too-easy and dualistic interpretation of this sermon.<|8.36|> <|9.04|> Perhaps some of you have heard it before.<|11.34|> <|11.64|> I know I have when I used to sit where you sit.<|15.34|> <|16.42|> And one that in my experience has caused many simply to stop listening, my husband being<|22.74|> <|22.74|> one for sure.<|23.52|> <|23.52|> Amen.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And that interpretation holds that there is virtue in poverty and vice in wealth.<|10.28|> <|11.48|> And there is so much scholarship from people who are so much smarter than I<|18.48|> <|18.48|> that support that Jesus does not despise the wealthy.<|24.32|> <|24.32|> Right?<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Hear that again.<|1.58|> <|3.16|> Jesus does not despise the wealthy.<|6.86|> <|8.60|> We see that especially in Luke, where Jesus has a close relationship with Zacchaeus,<|15.14|> <|15.14|> and Jesus tells the parable of the prodigal son wherein there is no judgment on the father for all that he has.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In fact, he is most often interpreted as the hero of that parable.<|6.46|> <|8.04|> Yes, blessed are the poor, for they will get theirs in the kingdom.<|13.34|> <|14.48|> Yes, blessed are the hungry, for they will be filled.<|17.76|> <|18.94|> Yes, blessed are those who weep, for they will laugh.<|23.28|> <|23.28|> Amen.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But Luke, in this gospel, Jesus wants to make sure that the wealthy, the privileged, don't stop listening there.<|14.38|> <|15.28|> What follows are the woes or the warnings or the woes, if you will.<|23.16|> <|23.16|> Amen.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> these are not pronouncements or curses.<|5.04|> <|6.22|> And I wonder if it has something to do with my role here at the cathedral as canon pastor<|11.16|> <|11.16|> that I am disposed to characterize them as Christ's pastoral concerns for his flock,<|19.12|> <|19.84|> who are not suffering.<|21.86|> <|21.86|> Amen.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It is Jesus' message, his version of let me save you from yourselves and remind you how<|9.82|> <|9.82|> you play a role in the kingdom of God breaking in here and now.<|17.56|> <|18.80|> You see, for Luke, the kingdom of God was not some far-off promise, but it was not a<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Luke was participate in the work of God in the world.<|21.98|> <|21.98|> Amen.<|22.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> An invitation to remember that while things might be going well for you right now,<|6.74|> <|7.32|> that may not always be the case.<|10.38|> <|11.28|> It keeps us from othering those who are having a different experience than we are having.<|18.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I think of our Book of Common Prayer service of Compline that asks for protection for all who work and weep, who are weary, sick, or dying.<|12.10|> <|12.98|> And then comes the zinger, the line that always haunts me, and shield the joyous.<|22.20|> <|22.20|> Amen.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Aren't we, my friends, also at some of our most vulnerable when all seems to be going well?<|12.00|> <|12.00|> We can lose sight in that time of our need for God, and we often lose sight of the needs of those around us.<|25.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We can be made vulnerable by our own joy and by our own present abundance.<|9.02|> <|10.26|> We can easily forget the invitation that is always right there, that invitation that is always there for the taking.<|19.32|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To be partners with God in bringing about the kingdom.<|4.32|> <|4.94|> In the upending of societal injustices now, not in some far off time.<|12.76|> <|12.76|> And by situating this teaching on the plane, Luke is literally leveling the playing field.<|23.00|> and abundance and liberation for everyone.<|25.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In our comfort, and I would venture that most of us right now are sitting in a place of comfort,<|9.76|> <|10.38|> we are asked to sit in discomfort.<|14.32|> <|15.32|> For the woes are not comfortable.<|19.08|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> all.<|1.02|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> However, if we do not shut them out, stop listening, domesticate them, or turn them into some annoyance,<|9.16|> <|9.70|> if we do not take them as <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The invitation is to partner in the holy work of kingdom building,<|5.36|> <|5.80|> to acknowledge that the kingdom of God bears little resemblance to
